question: Analyse how misaligned scheduling of pipeline builds contributes to unnecessary compute cost, and synthesise a scheduling optimisation strategy.
answer:
How misaligned scheduling creates unnecessary cost:

Scenario 1  -  Over-frequent scheduling:
A pipeline is scheduled every 15 minutes but its source data only updates once per hour. Builds 2, 3, and 4 in each hour find no new data but still spin up Spark executors, read input metadata, and write an identical output transaction. These builds incur full startup and overhead costs with zero data value.

Scenario 2  -  Dependency ignorance:
Pipeline B is scheduled 5 minutes after pipeline A, but on some days pipeline A takes 30 minutes. Pipeline B runs before A finishes and finds stale input; it produces incorrect output and must be manually re-triggered, consuming double the compute.

Scenario 3  -  Peak hour clustering:
Most pipelines are scheduled at the top of the hour (00 minutes) because that is the default; compute demand spikes at :00 and is idle at :30. On-demand cluster pricing charges more during peaks.

Scheduling optimisation strategy:

1. Data-triggered builds: replace time-based scheduling with data-triggered scheduling; a pipeline builds only when its source dataset has a new transaction since the last build. Eliminates empty builds.

2. Dependency-aware scheduling: use Foundry's build dependency graph rather than time-based offsets; pipeline B is triggered when pipeline A's build completes successfully, not N minutes after A starts.

3. Schedule spreading: stagger scheduled builds across the hour; assign each pipeline a random minute offset during initial setup. Reduces peak compute demand without changing total compute consumed.

4. SLA-driven minimum frequency: define the minimum refresh frequency required per pipeline (freshness SLA); do not schedule more frequently than the SLA requires. Document the SLA; any change to the schedule must be justified against the SLA.

Expected savings: eliminating empty builds typically reduces compute cost by 20-40% for pipelines with variable source update frequency.
justification: Scheduling optimisation is a practical cost reduction lever that requires analysing the mismatch between schedule frequency and actual data update patterns.

question: Analyse the hidden costs of maintaining unused or rarely-used datasets in Foundry, and synthesise a dataset lifecycle management policy that reduces these costs.
answer:
Hidden costs of unused datasets:

1. Storage cost: unused datasets accumulate historical transactions; Foundry retains all transactions unless explicitly cleaned up. A 1 TB dataset with daily full-snapshot builds retains 365 TB of historical transactions after one year unless a retention policy limits this.

2. Compute cost: pipelines that produce unused datasets still build on schedule; if no downstream consumer reads the output, the compute is wasted.

3. Build graph overhead: the Foundry build scheduler evaluates dependencies across all pipelines including unused ones; a large number of idle pipelines adds overhead to the scheduling process.

4. Metadata cost: every dataset transaction generates Iceberg or Foundry internal metadata; unused datasets accumulate metadata that must be scanned during lineage and audit operations.

5. Governance cost: unused datasets must still be classified, access-controlled, and quality-monitored; they consume data governance team attention disproportionate to their value.

Dataset lifecycle management policy:

Active threshold: a dataset is Active if it has at least one downstream build triggered by it or at least one user accessing it in the last 30 days.

Dormant (30-90 days of inactivity): the producing pipeline is suspended; the dataset retains its last 3 transactions. The registered owner receives a weekly dormancy notification.

Archived (90 days of inactivity): the dataset is moved to cold storage tier; it is no longer included in the build graph. It remains queryable but read latency increases.

Deleted (180 days of inactivity or owner confirmation): the dataset is permanently deleted after a 30-day confirmation period during which the owner receives daily deletion warnings.

Override mechanism: datasets required for regulatory retention are tagged REGULATORY_HOLD and are exempt from the lifecycle policy.
justification: Dataset lifecycle management requires synthesising cost analysis, policy design, automated enforcement, and governance overrides into a complete system.

parts: Resource Manager for resource tracking; Cost Optimization vs capabilities, compute and estimates

question: Analyse the cost implications of AIP LLM usage at enterprise scale and identify the optimisation levers that reduce cost without degrading user-perceived quality.
answer:
Cost drivers for AIP LLM usage at scale:

1. System prompt re-transmission: every AIP Assist query re-sends the full system prompt (which may be 2,000-5,000 tokens) as input; at 10,000 queries/day this adds 20-50M tokens/day in system prompt overhead alone.
Optimisation: system prompt caching (KV cache reuse)  -  the same system prompt sent to the same model in rapid succession can have its KV computation cached; the provider charges 0 or reduced rate for cached tokens on the second and subsequent calls.

2. Context over-inclusion: AIP Logic functions include full Object Type records in the context when only 3 of 40 properties are relevant to the task.
Optimisation: selective context construction  -  only include properties that are relevant to the specific task; a 40-property record reduced to 5 properties cuts input token cost by ~87%.

3. Output verbosity: the model is prompted without output length constraints; it produces verbose responses when concise answers are sufficient.
Optimisation: include explicit output format and length instructions in the prompt; 'Respond in under 3 sentences' can reduce output tokens by 60-80% for summary tasks.

4. Model tier mismatch: large frontier models are used for simple classification tasks that a small fine-tuned model handles equally well.
Optimisation: route simple, high-volume tasks (classification, extraction, summarisation) to a smaller model tier; use the large model only for complex reasoning tasks.

5. Redundant calls: the same query is made multiple times across a session because the previous result was not cached.
Optimisation: implement a session-level cache keyed on (query_hash, context_hash); identical queries within a session return the cached result without an API call.

Quality impact monitoring: for each optimisation, measure user satisfaction ratings before and after; only retain optimisations where quality metrics do not degrade.
justification: LLM cost optimisation at enterprise scale requires analysing each independent cost driver and matching it to a targeted optimisation lever.

question: Synthesise a FinOps governance model for a 200-person Foundry platform where engineering teams, business analysts, and data scientists all have self-service pipeline creation capabilities.
answer:
FinOps governance model:

Principle: visibility precedes control. Engineers must be able to see the cost of their work before being constrained by it.

Layer 1  -  Cost attribution:
Every compute resource consumed is tagged with: team, project, pipeline_name, and environment (dev/staging/prod). Daily cost attribution reports are automatically sent to team leads with per-project and per-pipeline breakdowns.

Layer 2  -  Budget allocation:
Each team receives a monthly compute budget at the start of each month, based on the previous month's usage plus a 20% growth allowance. Budgets are held in a central FinOps dataset visible to all team leads.

Layer 3  -  Self-service guardrails:
- Dev environment: unlimited compute but with a 2-hour automatic build timeout and a 30-day data retention policy. No SLA requirements in dev.
- Staging environment: limited to 20% of the production compute profile; used for integration testing only.
- Production environment: requires a FinOps review for any pipeline expected to consume > $500/month (based on the estimate from Resource Manager). Review takes 2 business days.

Layer 4  -  Automated cost controls:
- Builds that exceed the per-run cost limit for their profile are automatically killed and the pipeline owner is alerted.
- Pipelines that have not produced a build consumed by any downstream pipeline in 30 days receive a dormancy notice.
- AIP usage per team is capped at the budget level; excess requests are queued for off-peak processing.

Layer 5  -  Monthly FinOps review:
A 60-minute cross-team review covers: top 5 cost increases this month, explanation and classification (expected growth, incident, inefficiency), and action items for inefficiency cases. Outputs feed into the next month's budget adjustments.
justification: Synthesising a FinOps governance model for a self-service platform requires integrating attribution, budgeting, guardrails, automated controls, and review processes across multiple user types.

parts: Resource Manager for resource tracking; Cost Optimization vs capabilities, compute and estimates

question: Analyse the cost trade-offs between storing data in hot, warm, and cold storage tiers in a Foundry MMDP deployment, and identify the criteria for assigning a dataset to each tier.
answer:
Storage tier definitions in Azure context:
- Hot tier (ADLS Gen2 hot): highest storage cost/GB/month; lowest read latency (milliseconds); lowest read transaction cost.
- Cool tier (ADLS Gen2 cool): ~50% lower storage cost than hot; slightly higher read latency; higher read transaction cost.
- Archive tier (ADLS Gen2 archive): ~95% lower storage cost than hot; read requires rehydration (hours to days); very high rehydration cost per GB.

Cost model at scale (approximate, Azure):
- Hot: $0.018/GB/month storage, $0.004/10,000 reads.
- Cool: $0.01/GB/month storage, $0.01/10,000 reads.
- Archive: $0.001/GB/month storage, $0.02/GB rehydration.
Break-even: a dataset read fewer than once per month costs less in cool tier; read fewer than once per 6 months costs less in archive tier (if rehydration latency is acceptable).

Tier assignment criteria:

Hot tier:
- Active gold-layer and Ontology-backing datasets (queried multiple times per day).
- Datasets backing real-time or near-real-time features.
- Any dataset with a sub-1-minute read SLA.

Warm/cool tier:
- Silver layer datasets used for batch processing runs (read daily or weekly).
- Historical snapshots used for periodic reporting (read 2-4 times per month).
- Staging and integration test datasets.

Cold/archive tier:
- Raw bronze data older than 90 days (ingestion auditing only, never queried in normal operations).
- Superseded model versions and historical training datasets.
- Regulatory retention datasets (required to exist but not accessed unless audited).

Automated tiering: a lifecycle management pipeline runs weekly and moves datasets to the appropriate tier based on their last_accessed_date; the tier assignment is recorded in the data catalog for audit purposes.
justification: Storage tier assignment requires analysing the cost model and matching each dataset's access pattern to the appropriate tier  -  the foundational FinOps storage decision.

parts: Resource Manager for resource tracking; Cost Optimization vs capabilities, compute and estimates
value: Metrics on effectiveness and efficiency of workloads, governance and informed capacity planning
question: Synthesise a cost-aware pipeline design review process that engineering teams must follow before deploying any new production pipeline to a shared Foundry environment.
answer:
Review process:

Step 1  -  Cost estimation (engineer, before review):
The pipeline author uses Foundry's Resource Manager to estimate: compute profile required, estimated build duration at current data volume, estimated storage growth per month, estimated number of builds per day.
The author fills in a one-page cost estimation template: {pipeline_name, data_volume_gb, build_frequency, compute_profile, estimated_monthly_compute_cost_usd, estimated_monthly_storage_cost_usd, total_estimated_monthly_cost_usd}.

Step 2  -  Business value justification:
The author documents: {business_question_answered, consuming_teams, number_of_users_benefiting, data_freshness_required, why_this_freshness_is_required}.
If the estimated monthly cost exceeds $500, the justification must include: a cost-per-decision-supported calculation and a comparison to the cost of the manual process being replaced.

Step 3  -  Architecture review:
A FinOps engineer reviews the cost estimate and the pipeline design for:
- Is the build frequency higher than the data freshness SLA requires?
- Is the compute profile larger than the data volume warrants?
- Is the pipeline a full-snapshot when incremental would suffice?
- Are intermediate datasets produced that no downstream pipeline consumes?

Step 4  -  Approval tiers:
- < $200/month: self-approved by team lead.
- $200-$1,000/month: FinOps engineer approval required.
- > $1,000/month: FinOps engineer + platform director approval.

Step 5  -  Post-deployment review at 30 days:
The pipeline is reviewed at 30 days: actual cost vs estimate, actual consuming teams vs planned. If actual cost exceeds estimate by > 50%, the pipeline is flagged for redesign review.

Step 6  -  Annual renewal:
Every pipeline with a monthly cost > $500 is reviewed annually; the business value justification is refreshed. Pipelines whose business value is no longer current are decommissioned.
justification: A cost-aware design review process requires synthesising cost estimation, business justification, architectural review, approval tiers, and post-deployment monitoring into a complete governance workflow.

parts: Resource Manager for resource tracking; Cost Optimization vs capabilities, compute and estimates

question: Analyse the cost efficiency trade-offs of spot/preemptible instances versus on-demand instances for Foundry Spark workloads.
answer:
Spot/preemptible instances: the cloud provider can reclaim these instances at short notice (typically 2 minutes' warning on GCP, 2 minutes on Azure Spot). Cost is 60-90% lower than equivalent on-demand instances.

On-demand instances: guaranteed availability for the duration of the build; not reclaimed mid-execution. Cost is the baseline rate.

How Spark handles spot instance reclamation:
- When an executor is reclaimed, Spark detects the lost executor via heartbeat timeout.
- Spark re-schedules the tasks that were running on the lost executor to remaining executors.
- If the lost executor held shuffle map output, the dependent stages must re-read the input and recompute the shuffle output  -  adding significant time to the build.

Cost-efficiency analysis:
- For a 4-hour build on spot instances at 70% discount: if the build experiences 1 executor loss, the recomputation adds ~30 minutes; net time is 4.5 hours but cost is still 0.7 * 4.5 hours = effectively still cheaper than 4 hours on demand.
- For a 30-minute critical-path build: a spot interruption that adds 20 minutes represents a 67% latency increase; the SLA may be violated even though cost is lower.

When spot instances are cost-effective:
- Long-running, fault-tolerant batch jobs with soft SLAs (data science training, large historical backfills).
- Jobs where the shuffle output is checkpointed to persistent storage before each stage, reducing recomputation cost on interruption.
- Dev and staging environments where latency SLAs are not enforced.

When spot instances are not appropriate:
- Production gold-layer pipelines with hard freshness SLAs.
- Pipelines feeding real-time operational dashboards or Ontology-backed user-facing features.
- Short builds (< 20 minutes) where the interruption overhead is proportionally very high.

Hybrid strategy: use a mixed fleet  -  a small number of on-demand executors as a stable base; additional spot executors for burst capacity. The on-demand base maintains progress even during interruptions.
justification: Spot instance cost-efficiency analysis requires understanding Spark's failure recovery behaviour and matching the resilience characteristics of the workload to the interruption risk profile.

parts: Resource Manager for resource tracking; Cost Optimization vs capabilities, compute and estimates
value: Metrics on effectiveness and efficiency of workloads, governance and informed capacity planning
question: Analyse the cost and quality trade-offs of reducing data retention periods for bronze-layer raw data, and synthesise a retention policy that balances cost, regulatory compliance, and reprocessing capability.
answer:
Cost of long retention:
- Storage cost grows linearly with retention period; retaining bronze data for 3 years instead of 1 year triples the storage cost for that layer.
- For a platform ingesting 100 GB/day of raw data, 3 years of retention = 109 TB; at $0.018/GB/month for hot storage, this is $1,962/month storage cost for bronze alone.

Value of long retention:
- Reprocessing capability: if a silver or gold transform bug is discovered 6 months after deployment, the bronze data allows the pipeline to be replayed correctly from the raw source. Without the bronze data, reprocessing requires re-ingestion from the source (which may no longer be available or may have changed).
- Audit and compliance: certain regulated data must be retained for 5-7 years (financial transaction data under SOX, healthcare data under HIPAA).
- Root cause analysis: when a production incident is traced to a specific data anomaly, the raw bronze data provides the original record for forensic investigation.

Synthesised retention policy:

Classification tier 1  -  Regulated data:
Retain in archive tier for the full regulatory period (5-7 years). Archive tier cost is ~95% lower than hot; the storage cost at archive tier for 3 years of 100 GB/day is ~$7/month.

Classification tier 2  -  Business-critical reprocessable data:
Retain in hot tier for 90 days (covers the window in which bugs are typically discovered in production); move to cool tier from 90-365 days; archive from 1-3 years; delete after 3 years.

Classification tier 3  -  Low-value operational data (logs, metrics raw events):
Retain in hot tier for 14 days (debugging window); delete after 14 days unless a specific incident requires extended retention.

Automated lifecycle: a daily Foundry pipeline applies the retention policy to all bronze datasets based on their registered classification; deletions require a 7-day warning period visible to the dataset owner.
justification: Retention policy synthesis requires balancing cost analysis, regulatory compliance, and operational reprocessing needs  -  a real enterprise data platform governance decision.

parts: Resource Manager for resource tracking; Cost Optimization vs capabilities, compute and estimates
value: Metrics on effectiveness and efficiency of workloads, governance and informed capacity planning
question: Synthesise a chargeback reporting system for a shared Foundry platform where compute and storage costs must be allocated back to 8 business units for internal billing purposes.
answer:
Architecture:

Layer 1  -  Resource tagging:
Every compute resource (Spark build, Function invocation, LLM API call) and every storage asset (dataset, Media Set) is tagged at creation with: business_unit, team, project, environment, and cost_category (ingestion, transformation, serving, ai_usage).
Tagging is enforced by platform policy: any resource created without all required tags is automatically tagged as 'UNTAGGED' and the creating team receives an alert.

Layer 2  -  Cost data collection:
Cloud provider cost and usage reports (Azure Cost Management or AWS Cost Explorer) are exported daily to a raw cost dataset in Foundry. Each row contains: resource_id, resource_type, tag_map, usage_quantity, unit_price, cost_usd, billing_date.

Layer 3  -  Cost attribution pipeline:
A daily Foundry pipeline joins the raw cost data with the resource tag map to produce an attributed cost dataset: {billing_date, business_unit, team, project, cost_category, compute_cost_usd, storage_cost_usd, ai_cost_usd, total_cost_usd}.
Shared infrastructure costs (platform services not attributable to a specific business unit) are allocated pro-rata by each business unit's share of total attributed costs.

Layer 4  -  Chargeback report generation:
A monthly Function generates a formal chargeback report per business unit: {month, business_unit, attributed_costs_by_category, shared_infra_allocation, total_chargeback_usd, prior_month_comparison, top_5_cost_drivers}.
Reports are distributed to business unit finance contacts by the 5th of each following month via an automated email generated from Workshop.

Layer 5  -  Dispute resolution:
Business units have 10 business days to dispute an allocation. Disputes are logged in an Ontology-backed tracker; the FinOps team investigates and either confirms or adjusts the allocation. Adjusted allocations are corrected in the following month's report.

Governance: UNTAGGED cost percentage is tracked monthly; the target is < 2% of total costs. Teams with UNTAGGED costs above 5% receive escalating governance responses.
justification: Synthesising a chargeback reporting system requires integrating resource tagging, cost data collection, attribution, report generation, and dispute resolution into a complete internal billing architecture.
